title: Mount Rushmore — brain-computer-interface idea (objective neural communication, legal-system implications, anti-government-access)
html:
TL;DR

  • Sept 15, 2022 idea-seed titled "Mount Rushmore" (likely the working name Yahir gave the would-be venture — a Mt-Rushmore-tier civilizational bet) for a brain-computer-interface that converts neural objective communication into transmittable signals.
  • The two-step product thesis: (1) seamless brainwave-to-brainwave communication, (2) legal-system applications — "can essentially read peoples minds" — and the immediate ethical guardrail: "Can't let the government access this tech."
  • Compresses the entire BCI debate into three lines: utility (telepathy), killer app (legal/courtroom truth-detection), and the surveillance state failure mode in the same breath.
  • One of the earliest in-vault entries in the idea-seed-then-walk-away genre — Yahir doesn't return to BCI in later notes, but the "seamless interface that removes the human bottleneck" template recurs as mindless human interface disruption in 2024.
  • Modified once at 10:50am the same day — the entire artifact is a 60-second flash thought, captured cleanly, never expanded.

Key fragment

"Figuring out neurons objective communication through brainwave type of tech. Leads to seamless communications, then had implications in the legal field. Can essentially read peoples minds. Can't let the government access this tech"

Why it matters

This is one of the first appearances of the anti-surveillance instinct in Yahir's idea-stream (later refined into the surveillance state concept after the China year). It's also a clean specimen of how Yahir's product thinking works at 19: a single sentence that holds (a) the technical wedge, (b) the killer app, (c) the failure mode that would kill it. He won't formalize this pattern for years, but it's already operating.

Open questions

  • Was "Mount Rushmore" an aspirational codename for the venture or a metaphor for "four giant ideas carved in stone"?
  • Did the BCI thread reappear post-Neuralink news cycles in 2024–2026? (Worth a search.)

Wikilinks
